title: A research repository that people actually use
summary: Turning a graveyard of research reports into a queryable knowledge base — used weekly by design, PM, and engineering.
constraints: Must be maintained by researchers themselves

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: The research team was producing excellent work no one could find. Reports lived in Notion, decks lived in Drive, insights lived in slack threads. This project turned that mess into a single home.

sections.heading: Problem
sections.body: In interviews, 8 out of 10 PMs said they'd 'probably re-run research that already existed'. The cost was not just wasted studies — it was decisions made without prior context.

sections.heading: Approach
sections.body: Insights, not reports, are the atomic unit. Each insight has a claim, a source, a confidence level, and a tag graph. The home surface is a query builder disguised as search: 'insights about onboarding, high confidence, from the last 6 months'.

sections.heading: Outcome
sections.body: Weekly viewers grew to nearly 200 people across three functions. PRDs began linking research inline. The research team stopped feeling like a service desk and started acting like a knowledge network.

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: Internal tools are the highest-leverage design work most companies never invest in. The ROI on a good one is measured in years.
